Party_Details.Name: Tan Eck Hong

Facts.Text: Tan Eck Hong and Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd were shareholders of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd before the dispute.

Around March 2005, discussions took place between Seeto Keong and Lim Chong Poon about incorporating Treasure Resort Pte Ltd with Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd as majority shareholder to take over a Sentosa lease and hotel from Sijori Resort (Sentosa) Pte Ltd.
On 28 June 2005, Treasure Resort Pte Ltd was incorporated and Seeto Keong became its chairman and one of its two directors.
On 28 June 2005, Chiang Sing Jeong became a director of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd.

On 19 September 2005, Tan Eck Hong and Treasure Resort Pte Ltd signed a non-binding memorandum of understanding recording his intention to purchase 8% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares for $720,000, with an 8.5% per annum yield based on $720,000 and one seat on the board of directors.
On 19 September 2005, the memorandum of understanding stated that Tan Eck Hong would invest $720,000 in Treasure Resort Pte Ltd based on an estimated valuation of the Sentosa lease at $9,000,000.

On 19 September 2005, a memorandum of understanding between Tan Eck Hong and Treasure Resort Pte Ltd provided that his $720,000 investment would be used for purchasing the leasehold interest in the Sijori resort property and for renovation, refurbishment and operational expenses.

On 29 September 2005, Seeto Keong and Treasure Resort Pte Ltd CEO Gary Koh wrote to assure Tan Eck Hong that his $720,000 investment in Treasure Resort Pte Ltd would be used in accordance with the 19 September 2005 memorandum of understanding.
By 29 September 2005, $720,000 from Tan Eck Hong had been made available to Treasure Resort Pte Ltd as an investment under the memorandum of understanding.
On 27 October 2005, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd transferred 65,600 Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares, representing 8% of the then issued 820,000 shares, to Tan Eck Hong.
On 27 October 2005, 65,600 Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares were registered in the name of Tan Eck Hong.
On 7 December 2005, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd received a loan of $160,000 from Tan Eck Hong, to be returned by 24 December 2005.

On 7 December 2005, a handwritten note titled the Letter Agreement acknowledged receipt of $160,000 from Mr EH Tan as a loan to Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd, to be returned by 24 December 2005, and stated that Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd would provide an additional 2% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares to his existing 8% shareholding.

On 7 December 2005, the Letter Agreement recorded that Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd would provide an additional 2% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares to Tan Eck Hong, with the share transfer to be exercised after 7 December 2005.
24 December 2005 was the stated repayment date for the $160,000 loan from Tan Eck Hong to Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd under the Letter Agreement.
On 8 August 2006, Seeto Keong, Tan Eck Hong and Chiang Sing Jeong entered into the First Shareholders’ Agreement relating to Treasure Resort Pte Ltd.
On 8 August 2006, the First Shareholders’ Agreement recorded that Tan Eck Hong held 1% of the shares of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd.

On 8 August 2006, the First Shareholders’ Agreement granted Tan Eck Hong veto rights over specified shareholder matters requiring unanimous consent, including amendments to the memorandum or articles of association, changes in shareholders, and appointment or removal of directors.
On 8 August 2006, the First Shareholders’ Agreement granted Tan Eck Hong veto rights over specified board management matters, including payment of compensation for loss of office.

On 10 August 2006, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd executed a Declaration of Trust in favour of Chiang Sing Jeong relating to Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares; Tan Eck Hong was not a party or beneficiary.

On 31 October 2006, a Call Option Agreement was signed by Seeto Keong on behalf of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd granting Tan Eck Hong an option to purchase 6% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares from Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd for one cent, in return for one cent option fee.

On 31 October 2006, Tan Eck Hong paid one cent as consideration for an option to purchase 6% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares from Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd for one cent under the Call Option Agreement.
On 5 December 2006, Seeto Keong, Chiang Sing Jeong and Tan Eck Hong entered into a Supplemental Agreement to the First Shareholders’ Agreement.

On 5 December 2006, the Supplemental Agreement acknowledged that Tan Eck Hong was entitled to 16% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares based on the memorandum of understanding (8%), the Letter Agreement (2%) and the Call Option Agreement (6%).
On 14 November 2006, Treasure Resort Pte Ltd acquired the Sentosa lease and hotel from Sijori Resort (Sentosa) Pte Ltd with the consent of Sijori and Sentosa Development Corporation.

On 14 November 2006, Treasure Resort Pte Ltd entered into an agreement with Sentosa Development Corporation to lease an adjoining property and undertook to renovate the hotel and extend it with a new wing.
By May 2007, Treasure Resort Pte Ltd and its expansion project lacked funds for renovation and construction and were described as being on the verge of collapse.

By May 2007, discussions took place for Rodney Tan Boon Kian, through Roscent Group Ltd, to acquire more than 50% of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd shares to obtain majority control of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd and Treasure Resort Pte Ltd.

On 11 May 2007, the Second Shareholders’ Agreement dated 11 May 2007 was prepared to regulate the position between Tan Eck Hong and Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd following termination of the First Shareholders’ Agreement.
On 11 May 2007, the Second Shareholders’ Agreement was signed by Seeto Keong on behalf of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd and by Tan Eck Hong.

On 11 May 2007, deeds were signed to terminate the First Shareholders’ Agreement, the Supplemental Agreement, the memorandum of understanding, the Letter Agreement, the Call Option Agreement and the Declaration of Trust.

On 11 May 2007, a meeting took place in a private room in a karaoke lounge attended by Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd solicitor Allister Lim, Seeto Keong, Chiang Sing Jeong and Tan Eck Hong, where the Second Shareholders’ Agreement and termination deeds were explained and signed.
On 11 May 2007, Rodney Tan Boon Kian was present in the room when the Second Shareholders’ Agreement and related termination deeds were explained and signed.

Under the Second Shareholders’ Agreement dated 11 May 2007, Tan Eck Hong agreed to reduce his shareholding in Treasure Resort Pte Ltd to 13% and to give up veto rights contained in clauses 5.3 and 6 of the First Shareholders’ Agreement, a non-dilution clause maintaining his 13% shareholding up to issued capital of $6.2 million.
On 11 May 2007, Treasure Resort Pte Ltd had issued 4,820,001 shares with a par value of $1 each.

On 11 May 2007, a deed of termination and release relating to the memorandum of understanding recorded that Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd agreed to transfer 626,000 Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares to Tan Eck Hong.

On 11 May 2007, a share transfer form for 561,000 Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares in favour of Tan Eck Hong was executed by Seeto Keong and affixed with the corporate seal of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd, witnessed by solicitor Allister Lim.

On 17 May 2007, solicitor Pebble Sia wrote to solicitor Allister Lim requesting confirmation of Tan Eck Hong’s appointment as director of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd with effect from 11 May 2007 and confirmation that the shares in question had been transferred to him with effect from 11 May 2007, together with relevant share certificates.
On 30 May 2007, a meeting took place between Rodney Tan Boon Kian, Tan Eck Hong, Chiang Sing Jeong and Seeto Keong to discuss a further adjustment of their shareholdings in Treasure Resort Pte Ltd.
On 30 May 2007, an agreement was reached that Tan Eck Hong would transfer 1% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares to Chiang Sing Jeong.

On 11 June 2007, Rodney Tan Boon Kian sent an email to, among others, solicitor Pebble Sia, Seeto Keong and Gary Koh stating that the planned shareholder structure of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd would be Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd 86%, Tan Eck Hong 12%, and Chiang Sing Jeong 2%.

On 2 August 2007, Rodney Tan Boon Kian sent a text message to Tan Eck Hong stating that he did not want Sentosa Development Corporation to question Tan Eck Hong’s stake in Treasure Resort Pte Ltd and that after a meeting and commencement of a first loan, he would start procedures from extraordinary general meetings of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd and Treasure Resort Pte Ltd to seek consent to appoint Tan Eck Hong as director and transfer shares to him.

On 11 September 2007, Tan Eck Hong emailed Rodney Tan Boon Kian stating that under the 11 May agreement Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd needed to transfer Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares to maintain his 13% up to $6,200,000 paid-up capital and calculated that, based on his then shareholding, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd needed to transfer another 743,000 shares to him; he also asked about Sentosa Development Corporation approval for his directorship.
On 12 September 2007, Rodney Tan Boon Kian replied to Tan Eck Hong’s email about his entitlement to 13% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares with the words "As discussed".

On 12 September 2007, Tan Eck Hong emailed Rodney Tan Boon Kian referring to being allowed to look at unaudited accounts, a proposal to buy shares at $1.00 capitalisation price and set off the $160,000 loan, and the timing of Sentosa Development Corporation’s approval for a Maybank loan and for his directorship appointment.
On 19 February 2008, Tan Eck Hong swore a statutory declaration stating that he was the legal and beneficial owner of 65,600 Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares.
On 19 February 2008, the statutory declaration by Tan Eck Hong did not mention any entitlement to additional Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares under the Second Shareholders’ Agreement.
By November 2008, Tan Eck Hong commenced Suit No 898 of 2008 against Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd seeking specific performance of the Second Shareholders’ Agreement.
In 2007, Tan Eck Hong also commenced Suit No 581 of 2007 concerning oppression of the minority under section 216 of the Companies Act in relation to Treasure Resort Pte Ltd.
As at 11 May 2007, if Tan Eck Hong held 13% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares, this would correspond to 626,600 shares out of 4,820,001 issued shares.
As at 11 May 2007, taking into account 65,600 shares already registered in his name, a further 561,000 Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares would bring Tan Eck Hong’s total to approximately 626,600 shares.
Under the non-dilution clause of the Second Shareholders’ Agreement, 13% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares capped at $6.2 million issued capital corresponded to 806,000 shares.

Based on a target of 806,000 shares for 13% under the non-dilution clause and 65,600 shares already allotted, Tan Eck Hong calculated that Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd would need to transfer 740,400 Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares to him.
After 11 May 2007, the issued share capital of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd was increased to exceed $13 million.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute agreements and share transfer documentation, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd was transfer additional Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares to maintain Tan Eck Hong’s agreed percentage shareholding.
Party_Details.Rule: In shareholding arrangements, a written shareholders’ agreement and related instruments can define parties’ respective share entitlements and may be specifically enforceable, particularly for shares in a private company that are not freely available on the open market, where consideration consists of monetary investment, loans, or renunciation of contractual rights.
Party_Details.Application: Tan Eck Hong invested $720,000 under a memorandum of understanding referencing 8% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares and later provided a $160,000 loan to Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd documented in the Letter Agreement referencing an additional 2% of shares. The First Shareholders’ Agreement and Supplemental Agreement subsequently recorded his entitlement to a larger percentage of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares and granted him veto rights. On 11 May 2007, the Second Shareholders’ Agreement was signed, contemporaneously with termination deeds for earlier documents, and recorded that his shareholding would be 13% with a non-dilution mechanism up to $6.2 million issued capital. A share transfer form for 561,000 shares and a deed recital for 626,000 shares were executed in his favour, and later communications from Rodney Tan referred to structures in which he held 12% or 13% and to procedures to transfer shares and appoint him as director. These pre-dispute events and documents form the basis for treating the Second Shareholders’ Agreement as defining his target shareholding and the number of shares to be transferred by Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed whether the Second Shareholders’ Agreement was valid and enforceable, addressing Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd’s arguments that Seeto Keong lacked authority, that he acted in breach of fiduciary duties, and that there was no consideration. Applying the rule in Royal British Bank v Turquand, the judge held that an outsider like Tan Eck Hong could assume internal company procedures were complied with and found that Seeto had at least apparent authority as managing director and CEO, especially given the involvement of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd’s solicitors in preparing and presenting the agreement. On fiduciary duties, the court held that Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd bore the burden of proving a breach and that it had not done so, noting the absence of any action by the company against Seeto and drawing an adverse inference from the decision not to call him as a witness. The judge rejected the contention that accommodating Rodney Tan’s investment conditions justified disregarding prior commitments to other parties and found that Rodney was aware of the Second Shareholders’ Agreement, relying on his presence at the 11 May 2007 signing, the 30 May 2007 share reallocation discussions, his 11 June 2007 email referencing a 12% shareholding for Tan Eck Hong, and his 2 August 2007 text message about starting procedures to transfer shares. On consideration, the court held that even if the historical documents were unenforceable, they created commercial expectations that could constitute consideration, and further found that Tan Eck Hong had given up legal rights under the Letter Agreement, the Call Option Agreement, and the First Shareholders’ Agreement, including veto rights and higher share entitlements. The judge accepted that the 2% under the Letter Agreement and the 8% under the memorandum of understanding, as recognised in the First Shareholders’ Agreement and Supplemental Agreement, reflected enforceable rights or at least rights capable of being compromised. The court concluded that the Second Shareholders’ Agreement was supported by consideration and was valid and binding on Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd. Regarding the statutory declaration of 19 February 2008, the judge accepted Tan Eck Hong’s explanation that Rodney requested it for Sentosa Development Corporation and that it did not amount to a waiver of his rights under the Second Shareholders’ Agreement; the court held that Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd could not rely on that document to avoid its obligations. On the remedy, the court considered the principles governing specific performance, including the nature of shares in a private company and the contractual clause in Article 6.3 of the Second Shareholders’ Agreement in which the parties agreed that specific performance could be sought and that no defence of adequate remedy at law would be raised. The judge found that specific performance would do more complete justice than damages, particularly because the number of shares to be transferred would affect the percentage equity in Treasure Resort Pte Ltd in light of other proceedings concerning share cancellations. The court found no undue delay by Tan Eck Hong in commencing proceedings and no hardship to Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd in performing a bargain concluded with legal advice. The court therefore ordered specific performance of the Second Shareholders’ Agreement, requiring Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd to transfer 740,400 Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares to Tan Eck Hong, and declined to order his appointment as director in this suit because that relief had been expressly abandoned at trial in light of a concurrent oppression action. The counterclaim by Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd for declarations that it was not bound by the Letter Agreement and Call Option Agreement was dismissed, and costs were awarded to Tan Eck Hong.

Party_Details.Name: Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Limited

Facts.Text: Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd was a shareholder of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd before the dispute.

In March 2005, Seeto Keong was acting as managing director and chief executive officer of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd and was involved in plans to use Treasure Resort Pte Ltd to take over a Sentosa lease and hotel.

In March 2005, discussions took place between Seeto Keong and Sijori Resort (Sentosa) Pte Ltd’s managing director Lim Chong Poon about Treasure Resort Pte Ltd taking over the Sentosa lease and hotel with Sentosa Development Corporation’s consent.
On 28 June 2005, Treasure Resort Pte Ltd was incorporated with Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd as its majority shareholder.
By 2004, Sijori Resort (Sentosa) Pte Ltd owed $12 million to the Bank of China.

In November 2005, Sentosa Development Corporation commenced legal proceedings against Sijori Resort (Sentosa) Pte Ltd to recover more than $1 million, creating a risk that the Sentosa lease would be forfeited.

On 19 September 2005, a memorandum of understanding recorded that Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd, as majority shareholder of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd, would benefit from an investment of $720,000 by Tan Eck Hong intended for the lease purchase and resort expenses.

On 29 September 2005, a letter signed by Seeto Keong and Gary Koh stated that the $720,000 investment from Tan Eck Hong would be used in accordance with the memorandum of understanding, including for the purchase price of the lease and renovation and operational expenses.
On 27 October 2005, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd transferred 65,600 Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares to Tan Eck Hong, representing 8% of the then issued 820,000 shares.

Following the 19 September 2005 memorandum of understanding, 720,000 new Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares were issued to Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd using the $720,000 provided by Tan Eck Hong.
By early December 2005, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd was short of funds and requested a $160,000 loan from Tan Eck Hong for three weeks.
On 7 December 2005, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd received a $160,000 loan from Tan Eck Hong, with a stated repayment date of 24 December 2005.

On 7 December 2005, the Letter Agreement signed on behalf of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd acknowledged the $160,000 loan from Mr EH Tan and stated that Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd would provide an additional 2% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares to his existing 8% shareholding.
24 December 2005 was the stated date for Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd to return the $160,000 loan to Tan Eck Hong under the Letter Agreement.
By 31 October 2006, the $160,000 loan from Tan Eck Hong to Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd remained unpaid for more than 10 months.
On 8 August 2006, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd entered into the First Shareholders’ Agreement with Tan Eck Hong and Chiang Sing Jeong concerning Treasure Resort Pte Ltd.
On 8 August 2006, the First Shareholders’ Agreement recorded that Tan Eck Hong held 1% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares and granted him veto rights over specified shareholder and board decisions.
On 10 August 2006, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd executed a Declaration of Trust in favour of Chiang Sing Jeong affecting its legal ownership of certain Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares.
On 31 October 2006, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd, through Seeto Keong, granted Tan Eck Hong a Call Option Agreement for 6% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares for one cent.

On 5 December 2006, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd, Tan Eck Hong and Chiang Sing Jeong entered into a Supplemental Agreement to the First Shareholders’ Agreement acknowledging that Tan Eck Hong was entitled to 16% of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares based on earlier arrangements.

On 14 November 2006, Treasure Resort Pte Ltd acquired the Sentosa lease and hotel with the consent of Sijori Resort (Sentosa) Pte Ltd and Sentosa Development Corporation, and entered into a lease for adjoining land with Sentosa Development Corporation.

By May 2007, Treasure Resort Pte Ltd lacked funds to renovate the hotel or commence construction on the expansion project, and both the company and the project were described as being on the verge of collapse.

By May 2007, Seeto Keong invited Rodney Tan Boon Kian to invest in Treasure Resort Pte Ltd, and Rodney indicated he would invest only if he obtained majority control of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd and Treasure Resort Pte Ltd.
By May 2007, Seeto Keong offered to sell more than 50% of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd shares to Roscent Group Ltd, a company controlled by Rodney Tan Boon Kian.

In 2007, Rodney Tan Boon Kian instructed Stamford Law Corporation to check whether Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd was the legal owner of all Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares registered in its name.

Stamford Law Corporation advised Rodney Tan Boon Kian that the First Shareholders’ Agreement dated 8 August 2006 and the 10 August 2006 Declaration of Trust appeared to affect Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd’s legal ownership of certain Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares.
Following legal advice, Rodney Tan Boon Kian instructed Stamford Law Corporation to arrange for termination of the First Shareholders’ Agreement and the Declaration of Trust.
Around the same period, Seeto Keong made arrangements to terminate the memorandum of understanding, the Letter Agreement and the Call Option Agreement involving Tan Eck Hong.

For termination of the agreements concerning him, Tan Eck Hong was represented by solicitor Pebble Sia of Esquire Law Corporation, who liaised with Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd’s solicitor, Allister Lim.

On 11 May 2007, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd’s solicitor, Allister Lim, attended a meeting with Seeto Keong, Chiang Sing Jeong and Tan Eck Hong, bringing the Second Shareholders’ Agreement and deeds to terminate the First Shareholders’ Agreement, the memorandum of understanding, the Letter Agreement, the Call Option Agreement and the Declaration of Trust.

On 11 May 2007, the Second Shareholders’ Agreement was signed on behalf of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd by Seeto Keong and by Tan Eck Hong, and termination deeds for the earlier agreements were executed.

Recitals C and D of the Second Shareholders’ Agreement stated that the shareholders of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd intended to terminate the First Shareholders’ Agreement with effect from 11 May 2007 and that contemporaneously the parties wished to enter into the Second Shareholders’ Agreement to regulate their rights and obligations in relation to their shareholding.

On 11 May 2007, a deed of termination and release relating to the memorandum of understanding recorded that Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd, as majority shareholder of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd, agreed to transfer 626,000 of its shares in Treasure Resort Pte Ltd to Tan Eck Hong.

On 11 May 2007, a share transfer form for 561,000 Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares in favour of Tan Eck Hong was executed by Seeto Keong on behalf of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd, with the company seal affixed and witnessed by solicitor Allister Lim.
On 11 May 2007, Treasure Resort Pte Ltd had issued 4,820,001 shares with a par value of $1 each, and 13% of this issued capital corresponded to approximately 626,600 shares.

On 17 May 2007, solicitor Pebble Sia wrote to solicitor Allister Lim requesting confirmation that the shares in question had been transferred to Tan Eck Hong with effect from 11 May 2007 and asking for the original share certificate evidencing his holdings and the cancelled share certificate of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd.

On 30 May 2007, Rodney Tan Boon Kian, Tan Eck Hong, Chiang Sing Jeong and Seeto Keong met to discuss a further adjustment of their shareholdings in Treasure Resort Pte Ltd, including a proposed transfer of 1% of shares from Tan Eck Hong to Chiang Sing Jeong.

On 11 June 2007, Rodney Tan Boon Kian emailed, among others, solicitor Pebble Sia, stating that the planned shareholder structure of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd would be Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd 86%, Tan Eck Hong 12%, and Chiang Sing Jeong 2%, and asked that Sentosa Development Corporation be informed that no new shareholders were expected.

On 2 August 2007, Rodney Tan Boon Kian texted Tan Eck Hong that he did not want Sentosa Development Corporation to question Tan Eck Hong’s stake in Treasure Resort Pte Ltd and that after a meeting and commencement of a first loan, he would start procedures from extraordinary general meetings of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd and Treasure Resort Pte Ltd to seek consent to appoint him as director and transfer shares to him.

On 11 September 2007, Tan Eck Hong emailed Rodney Tan Boon Kian asserting that under the 11 May agreement Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd needed to transfer Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares to maintain his 13% up to $6,200,000 paid-up capital and asked about Sentosa Development Corporation’s approval for his directorship.
On 12 September 2007, Rodney Tan Boon Kian replied to Tan Eck Hong’s email about his 13% entitlement with the words "As discussed".

On 12 September 2007, Tan Eck Hong emailed Rodney Tan Boon Kian referring to viewing unaudited accounts, buying shares at $1.00 capitalisation price with set-off of the $160,000 loan, and the expected timing of Sentosa Development Corporation’s approval for a Maybank loan and his directorship appointment.
After 11 May 2007, the issued share capital of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd was increased and eventually exceeded $13 million.
In 2007, Roscent Group Ltd, controlled by Rodney Tan Boon Kian, acquired Seeto Keong’s interest in Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd and became its new majority shareholder.

After Roscent Group Ltd became the majority shareholder of Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd, the company’s position on Tan Eck Hong’s entitlement to Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares changed and it refused to recognise the Second Shareholders’ Agreement and several earlier agreements.
By November 2008, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd was named as defendant in Suit No 898 of 2008 in which Tan Eck Hong sought specific performance of the Second Shareholders’ Agreement.

In its defence strategy, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd contended that the memorandum of understanding, the Letter Agreement, the Call Option Agreement and the First Shareholders’ Agreement were unenforceable and that the Second Shareholders’ Agreement could not be relied on.
Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd filed a counterclaim seeking a declaration that it was not bound by the Letter Agreement and the Call Option Agreement.

Article 6.3 of the Second Shareholders’ Agreement provided that parties agreed it might be impossible to measure in money the damages from failure to perform certain obligations and that a party would be entitled to seek injunctive relief, including specific performance, and that no party would raise the defence of an adequate remedy at law.

On 19 February 2008, at Rodney Tan Boon Kian’s request, Tan Eck Hong executed a statutory declaration for Sentosa Development Corporation stating that he was the legal and beneficial owner of 65,600 Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, in light of internal authority, prior agreements, and subsequent shareholding communications, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd was bound to transfer additional Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares to Tan Eck Hong under the Second Shareholders’ Agreement.
Party_Details.Rule: A company may be bound by agreements executed by its officers where they have actual or apparent authority, and outsiders are generally entitled to assume compliance with internal management rules. Shareholders’ agreements and related instruments can define binding share transfer obligations, and specific performance is an available remedy for contracts to transfer shares in private companies where damages are inadequate.
Party_Details.Application: Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd, through its managing director and chief executive officer, entered into a series of documents with Tan Eck Hong, including the memorandum of understanding, the Letter Agreement, the Call Option Agreement, the First Shareholders’ Agreement, the Supplemental Agreement and, on 11 May 2007, the Second Shareholders’ Agreement. The Second Shareholders’ Agreement was prepared and presented by the company’s solicitor, signed by its managing director with the company seal on an accompanying share transfer form, and contemporaneously accompanied by termination deeds for earlier instruments. Legal advice from Stamford Law Corporation had identified the First Shareholders’ Agreement and Declaration of Trust as affecting Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd’s legal ownership of Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares, and steps were taken to regularise the position. After Roscent Group Ltd became the majority shareholder, Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd’s stance shifted, and it declined to recognise the Second Shareholders’ Agreement, arguing lack of authority, issues and absence of consideration. However, pre-dispute communications from Rodney Tan Boon Kian, including the 11 June 2007 email describing a structure of 86% for Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd, 12% for Tan Eck Hong and 2% for Chiang Sing Jeong, and the 2 August 2007 text message about starting procedures to transfer shares and appoint a director, reflected an operational assumption that Tan Eck Hong would hold a double-digit percentage stake. The Second Shareholders’ Agreement contained an express clause on specific performance, and the shares in question were in a private company not freely tradable on the open market. These circumstances framed the dispute over whether Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd was contractually obliged to transfer the calculated number of shares to maintain the agreed percentage for Tan Eck Hong.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considered Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd’s arguments that the Second Shareholders’ Agreement was not binding because Seeto Keong lacked authority, because he acted contrary to the company’s interests and fiduciary obligations, and because there was no consideration from Tan Eck Hong. Applying the rule in Royal British Bank v Turquand, the judge held that an outsider such as Tan Eck Hong could assume compliance with internal management requirements and that the company bore the burden of showing that Seeto lacked authority. The court found that Seeto, as managing director and chief executive officer, had at least apparent authority, particularly since the company’s own solicitor, Allister Lim, had prepared the Second Shareholders’ Agreement, liaised with Tan Eck Hong’s solicitor, and attended the signing with the company seal affixed to the share transfer form. On the fiduciary argument, the court held that Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd had to prove that Seeto’s actions were contrary to the company’s interests and that Tan Eck Hong knew or was put on inquiry. The judge noted that the company had not taken any action against Seeto, had chosen not to call him as a witness despite listing him, and invited an adverse inference that his evidence would not have supported its case. The court rejected the contention that accommodating Rodney Tan’s investment conditions justified disregarding prior commitments to other parties, observing that ignoring those commitments could itself expose the company to litigation. The judge found that Rodney was aware of the Second Shareholders’ Agreement and the new arrangements, relying on his presence at the 11 May 2007 signing, the 30 May 2007 share reallocation discussions, his 11 June 2007 email describing a structure with 12% for Tan Eck Hong, and his 2 August 2007 text message about starting procedures to transfer shares and appoint a director. On consideration, the court held that even if the memorandum of understanding and other historical documents were not fully enforceable, they created commercial expectations that could constitute consideration, and further that Tan Eck Hong had given up legal rights under the Letter Agreement, the Call Option Agreement and the First Shareholders’ Agreement, including veto rights and higher share entitlements. The judge accepted that the 2% under the Letter Agreement and the 8% under the memorandum of understanding, as recognised in the First Shareholders’ Agreement and Supplemental Agreement, reflected rights capable of being compromised and that the 6% under the Call Option Agreement also formed part of the bundle of rights renounced. The court concluded that the Second Shareholders’ Agreement was supported by consideration and was valid and binding on Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd. Regarding the statutory declaration of 19 February 2008, the judge accepted evidence that Rodney requested it for Sentosa Development Corporation and that it did not represent a waiver of rights under the Second Shareholders’ Agreement; the court held that Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd could not rely on that document to avoid its obligations. On remedy, the court applied principles governing specific performance, noting that shares in a private company are not freely available on the open market and that Article 6.3 of the Second Shareholders’ Agreement expressly contemplated specific performance and waived the defence of adequate remedy at law. The judge found that specific performance would provide more complete justice than damages, particularly because the number of shares to be transferred would affect percentage equity in the context of other proceedings concerning share cancellations. The court found no undue delay by Tan Eck Hong and no hardship to Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd in performing a bargain concluded with legal advice. The court therefore ordered specific performance of the Second Shareholders’ Agreement, requiring Maxz Universal Development Group Pte Ltd to transfer 740,400 Treasure Resort Pte Ltd shares to Tan Eck Hong, declined to order his appointment as director in this suit because that relief had been abandoned at trial, dismissed the company’s counterclaim seeking declarations that it was not bound by the Letter Agreement and Call Option Agreement, and awarded costs to Tan Eck Hong.
